words: This converation with Frances Vaughan Fry took place at her home in Kendrick,
Idaho on February 18, 1977. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.
FRANCES VAUGHAN FRY: Heavy snows. But you cant tell people, a lot of people
around here, you can't tell these people of the snow we used to have.
We used to have- well, it wasn't anything to have three or four feet.
And, oh, a lot of times it would, you know, blow, what do they call it?
Drift? Well, we had to go and get them drifts and everything out of
the way before we could get out. And I've saw times— we lived about a
half a mile, yeah, about a half a mile from the main road up on Cedar
Creek there. And we had to- the menfolks was going to take a- they had
abob, you know we had the double Bobsled and they just took the front
bobs and made a bob out of it and was coming to Kendrick. And they
started down the lane and the menfolks had to go ahead of the horses
and break the trail so they could get through, because it was so deep.
But I don't know what winter it was, it was a long time ago, well, land
we never thought anything of shoveling out road. And then one year
we had snow til way up in May. And there was a man that ran out of
hay, didn't have any hay for his stock, and the snow had crusted. And
everything could on the snow, the horses and the cattle and
pigs and everything was out. Everybody's cattle was out, all over the
country. Everybody had everybody else's cattlel And they drove right
across the ridge that would come into our place off the main road, they'd
come in kind of slantchways to the barn. They drove right in there with
a bobsled and got a load of hay and drove right back out over the tops
of the fence any everything. Now, people won't believe 1 Some of
these young honyaks around here, they won't believe it, when you tell
them that. They think you're just talking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Honyaks?

speaker: FVF
words: Yeah, honyaks. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: I've never really heard that,

speaker: FVF
words: Oh, that's a what we called some people.A They just won't believe things

speaker: FVF
words: like that. Why, it wasn't anything. We went one time, one time we

speaker: FVF
words: went- when my oldest son, I think, was just a baby, now that's been

speaker: FVF
words: sixty some years ago- we went over to our neighbor's and they had to

speaker: FVF
words: go down about three-quarters of a mile or approximately a mile, down

speaker: FVF
words: to their place and we had company and we were in the bobsled and the

speaker: FVF
words: mfcnfolks had to go- it took us over three hours to go that distance.

speaker: SS
words: How far?

speaker: SS
words: About- oh, it was about- possibly a mile down the lane and they had to

speaker: SS
words: go ahead and the road for the horses, so the horses could get

speaker: SS
words: down there with the sled.

speaker: SS
words: How would they break the road? Just walking along?

speaker: FVF
words: Yeah, they'd go ahead of the horses first and then the horses'd come

speaker: FVF
words: along. Course if the horses had to break the road, the horses'd leap,

speaker: FVF
words: you know jump. out when they walked and just kind of made the trail,

speaker: FVF
words: and then the horses could walk right in, and things like that. But we

speaker: FVF
words: done that many a time.

speaker: SS
words: Was there much time say in the winter, that you would not go anyplace,

speaker: SS
words: that you would have to stay at home?

speaker: FVF
words: No. We nearly always- oh, of course, there'd be days we wouldn't

speaker: FVF
words: get out for maybe a day or two, but then the neighbors always- see we

speaker: FVF
words: lived within possibly- well, it was a mile, some was a mile and a half

speaker: FVF
words: mile and a quarter, a mile, or something like that, or three-quarters

speaker: FVF
words: of a mile. get out and shovel out a road to the main road. Well,

speaker: FVF
words: we done that lots of times.

speaker: SS
words: I wonder what's happened that's changed the cold and snow pattern so

speaker: SS
words: much, that now we don't have the snow and winter that we used to.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, no, nothing like that. And then that one winter,

speaker: SS
words: lots of snow, too, that winter. That was the winter I don't remember

speaker: SS
words: what winter it was, other people could tell you the exact winter it

speaker: SS
words: was, but now we lived on one side of the neighborhood up there; we lived

speaker: SS
words: on the side out this way farther and our neighbor lived back farther

speaker: SS
words: towards$ lder Creek country, and it was always ten degrees colder over

speaker: SS
words: at his place. We had around thirty and he had forty degreesit

speaker: SS
words: was that cold. And oh, the stock and all that- the animals and every

speaker: SS
words: thing, we had to make- of course then, we used to thresh and have big

speaker: SS
words: stacks of straw. Those cattle'd go over there and in, burrow under,

speaker: SS
words: you know, and eat in, when they didn't have ashed. We had sheds for

speaker: SS
words: most of 'em, but then they was times when we didn't have sheds for all

speaker: SS
words: of 'em and the rest of 'em would go over there around these piles of

speaker: SS
words: straw and just get as near in as they could, you know, under that straw

speaker: SS
words: Jit was so cold. But that was the only winter that I remember that was

speaker: SS
words: so cold. We've had cold weather. Since we've been down here we've

speaker: SS
words: had some cold weather because my daughter and I used to walk fromwe

speaker: SS
words: lived out by the- where the schoolhouse is now, we lived out there.

speaker: SS
words: We were both of us working down here and we'd come down the railroad

speaker: SS
words: track. We used to walk down the railroad track. Well, lots of times

speaker: SS
words: it was twenty below when we'd walk down the railroad track.

speaker: SS
words: How far were you walking to town?

speaker: SS
words: Well, from out here the schoolhouse is into to town here, and we

speaker: SS
words: came down the railroad track. And there was lots of mornings it was

speaker: SS
words: twenty- Now. that twenty degrees didn't mean too muchl^There was no

speaker: SS
words: wind. If it was just quiet, but, oh, if there was a wind you'd just

speaker: SS
words: pretty near freeze to death. And that's the way it is, of course, now.

speaker: SS
words: We had a few cold days, even this winter we've had a few cool days,

speaker: SS
words: But it is nothing like we used to haveJ

speaker: SS
words: Did you used to have to worry about the stock getting too cold in the

speaker: SS
words: winter?

speaker: FVF
words: No. We had barns and sheds and all that they could someway get kind

speaker: FVF
words: of out of the cold. We had a big cow barn and a horse barn, and then

speaker: FVF
words: of course, we had our hog pens and all houses for them, and all. No,

speaker: FVF
words: the stock was pretty well taken care of as far as that was concerned.

speaker: FVF
words: Course, our hay was in the barn, and we could just feed 'em right out

speaker: FVF
words: of it. We never had any trouble. We had to maybe shovel our path to

speaker: FVF
words: the barn and like that. I know the. night that it was so awfully cold

speaker: FVF
words: up there- during the time that it was so awfully cold, so many people

speaker: FVF
words: lost all their chickens. Their chickens just froze right in the coops.

speaker: FVF
words: And I know my neighbor lady, she had built a new chickenhouse, and one

speaker: FVF
words: of those real cold nights, one of my children were born. And I just

speaker: FVF
words: fussed all night long because I thought, well, my chickens freeze

speaker: FVF
words: to death. And I'll tell you what I had my chickens in, and that was

speaker: FVF
words: an old building, oh, it was a pretty good size shed, and they roosted

speaker: FVF
words: in this old shed; it wasnit a real chickenhouse, they roosted in this

speaker: FVF
words: shed. But they was up here, right at the top- it was built like thisthe

speaker: FVF
words: roof was down like this and then they was pieces up here at the

speaker: FVF
words: top. Well, those chickens was all up on that- up there, and I never

speaker: FVF
words: lost a frozen chickenI But this lady that had this new chickenhouse

speaker: FVF
words: lost hers, because there was so much moisture in there, with the chickens

speaker: FVF
words: in there. There was so much moisture in there that they everyone of

speaker: FVF
words: 'em froze! Now, that's unbelieveable maybe, but that was so.

speaker: SS
words: Is that because it was built too tight?

speaker: FVF
words: It was . tight and chickens, I guess they kind of sweat or something

speaker: FVF
words: like that, and they just froze. But then there was a lot of people

speaker: FVF
words: that lost 'em. They had regular chickenhouses and they lost their

speaker: FVF
words: chickens. But these of mine was up there in the top and they never

speaker: FVF
words: froze. And I was worried because I knew they weren't fed or taken

speaker: FVF
words: care of that evening.

speaker: SS
words: Well, how come somebody else in the family didn't—

speaker: FVF
words: Well, they were there but nobody else tended the chickens but me. So

speaker: FVF
words: they didn't get any attention that time.

speaker: SS
words: Was that how you- that you had certain things you did, and only you

speaker: SS
words: did them?

speaker: FVF
words: No, there was things that they did but other people didn't think about

speaker: FVF
words: it, when it wasn't their- well, Mr. Fry wasn't any hand to do chores

speaker: FVF
words: as far as that concerned, and I managed the chores and things like

speaker: FVF
words: that.

speaker: SS
words: That's what I was thinking, I mean, I got the idea that a lot of farm

speaker: SS
words: families, that there were certain chores that the wife did and things

speaker: SS
words: that the husband did.

speaker: FVF
words: There was a lot of that, too, but, it was just like the milking, I al

speaker: FVF
words: ways did the milking. We had about five or six cows most of the time.

speaker: FVF
words: I did the milking until one of the boys got big enough to help me. And

speaker: FVF
words: then one of the boys helped me and one helped their Dad. I had two

speaker: FVF
words: boys pretty close together.

speaker: SS
words: Did that take very long to milk six cows?

speaker: FVF
words: No. I don't know, maybe anywhere from a half to an hour. We had some

speaker: FVF
words: good cows, too. We had some that— we had a cow, and there's not very

speaker: FVF
words: many people had a cow like that- we had one cow that we raised the fa

speaker: FVF
words: mily almost! with. She was a big cow, she weighed- well, when we sold

speaker: FVF
words: her she weighed 1,800 pounds. And she give the best milk that was ever

speaker: FVF
words: invented. And the children could tell if I put any other milk on the

speaker: FVF
words: table but this cow's milk. They'd say, 'What's the mat ter with,"-

speaker: FVF
words: now, let's see, what was her name? Muelly, Ibelieve we called her.

speaker: FVF
words: "What's the matter with Muelly?" We had a- well, at the time we had

speaker: FVF
words: shorthorns and we had jerseys and we had this- she was a big roan.

speaker: FVF
words: Meanest thing that ever walked on four legs, but she was the best cow

speaker: FVF
words: you ever saw. And one time there was afellow come up there to work

speaker: FVF
words: and when she first came fresh, she lost her first calf, and we didn't

speaker: FVF
words: know she'd lost it, the neighbor had bought her, she was had

speaker: FVF
words: sold to this neighbor. And so when she came fresh, she was down in

speaker: FVF
words: the canyon, this neighbor never got her and something got her calf.

speaker: FVF
words: Well, he didn't know it til it was too late and he just let her go,

speaker: FVF
words: so that before she calved the next time, why, we bought her back.

speaker: FVF
words: It was the year of election, all betting on this and everybody was selling something

speaker: FVF
words: or buy

speaker: FVF
words: ing something at that time and we bought that cow back. She was acow

speaker: FVF
words: then and she was^rreshen that spring. Well, she did, she freshened.

speaker: FVF
words: And, oh, she was the biggest thing ans she gave the most milk and all.

speaker: FVF
words: I swore up and down, my husband was no hand, he wouldn't milk. He just

speaker: FVF
words: wouldn't milk, and oh, sometimes he had to, buttery seldom. Ihad a

speaker: FVF
words: little Jersey and held always milk this little Jersey. But this old

speaker: FVF
words: big cow, she was the one that we depended on for many years. And so

speaker: FVF
words: she had her calfs. Well, she'd never been milked. Of course they'd

speaker: FVF
words: never milked her when she had her first calf, they'd never milked her.

speaker: FVF
words: So when we got her, she had the calf, we never milked her, we just put

speaker: FVF
words: three calves on her. We left three calves on her all summer and she

speaker: FVF
words: nursed these three calves. And then when wintertime come and we needed

speaker: FVF
words: some milk, cow milk, our other cows, Iguess were dried up. So we had

speaker: FVF
words: ahired boy there with me and he went to the, oh, it was when they was

speaker: FVF
words: having a fairfdown at Lewiston there. The regular fair that they al

speaker: FVF
words: ways had horses and cows and all things like that. He went to the

speaker: FVF
words: Fair and he took these three calves off this cow, and my land, it

speaker: FVF
words: wasn't but a day til this cow couldn't hardly walk, you know she had

speaker: FVF
words: a big bag and she couldn'. So, I told this boy, I said, "Well,

speaker: FVF
words: if you'll go with me out to the barn, I'll try to milk that cow."

speaker: FVF
words: Well, by the time he got back I was milking the cow. And she was just

speaker: FVF
words: real- she was kind of a, you know, nervous cow, and you couldn't change

speaker: FVF
words: milkers on her. She didn't want anybody else to milk her but the one

speaker: FVF
words: that was used to milking her, like me, I milked her all the time pos

speaker: FVF
words: sibly that I could. And nojbne else did. So, I milked her for years.

speaker: FVF
words: But that cow about once a month, she would kick. And we tried to put

speaker: FVF
words: these kickers on her, but they wouldn't hold her, she'd just- they just

speaker: FVF
words: went everywhere. So I'd just have to milk her and if sh e kicked me,

speaker: FVF
words: why, I'd take it! Well, we had had a man there that did try to milk her

speaker: FVF
words: without, you know, the kickers, and he went out there- and everytime

speaker: FVF
words: he went out to the barn we could hear him fighting that cow. He would

speaker: FVF
words: whip her he did everything he could do, and so finally I told my hus

speaker: FVF
words: band, I said, "I'll milk her." So I got to milking her. So I'd milk

speaker: FVF
words: her all-time, but once in a while when she took a notion to kick, and

speaker: FVF
words: she didn't kick to miss you, she'd kick to hit you, and you usually got

speaker: FVF
words: it!

speaker: SS
words: Really?

speaker: FVF
words: Yes sir. And I'll tell you what she gave- it was before your time-

speaker: FVF
words: I don't suppose you ever saw a square can coal oil, that we used

speaker: FVF
words: to come to town and get a five gallon can of coal oil for our lamps;

speaker: FVF
words: they were about that big square. Well, they held over five gallon.

speaker: FVF
words: Well, after the oil was out of 'em, we cleaned 'em up good, and that's

speaker: FVF
words: what I took for that cow's milk. I had a small bucket that I milked

speaker: FVF
words: in, and then I had this big bucket and I'd milk- many a day and day

speaker: FVF
words: after day, every time I milked that cow, I got that five gallon can

speaker: FVF
words: full.

speaker: SS
words: Every day?

speaker: FVF
words: Every day; twice a day. That five gallon can. And our neighbor was

speaker: FVF
words: up to our place and I went out to milk one morning and she says, "Well,

speaker: FVF
words: what you going to do with all them buckets?" I said, " Going to milk

speaker: FVF
words: the cow." He wouldn't believe it. Well, I did it, just the same.

speaker: FVF
words: And we kept that cow til, well, just- we kept her for years and years

speaker: FVF
words: and years. And finally she had twin calves, and then we had to get

speaker: FVF
words: rid of her.

speaker: SS
words: How come?

speaker: FVF
words: Well, she just didn't do any good, you know. We had toilet her go.

speaker: FVF
words: But she sure was a good cow. Now with the jersey; I had a separator,

speaker: FVF
words: cream separator. With the jersey cow you could run her milk through

speaker: FVF
words: the separator, well when you got the milk through the separator, there

speaker: FVF
words: was nothing left in it, it was just nothing but milk- there was no

speaker: FVF
words: cream in it at all. Well, this old cow's milk, you could separate it

speaker: FVF
words: and you could skim it, and the cream would raise on it always. Now

speaker: FVF
words: that was the kind of milk she gave.

speaker: SS
words: You said when you got that cow there, it was during an election and

speaker: SS
words: everybody was betting? Now, did you win that cow on a bet or did you-

speaker: FVF
words: No, no, we were just betting. I know we bet somebody bet you a colt

speaker: FVF
words: or somebody bet you this or bet you that. I think that was the year

speaker: FVF
words: that I had three steers- seems to me like I bought those steers- my

speaker: FVF
words: husband had- they were his, that is- possibly his, well, I bought those

speaker: FVF
words: three steers for my own doings and he bought this cow. That's how we

speaker: FVF
words: had the cow. He bought this cow back from this neighbor and I bought

speaker: FVF
words: these steers from him. And one steer I sold for over $100. One steer

speaker: FVF
words: died- and let's see now what happened to the other one- I got a $100

speaker: FVF
words: and one died, and there was something happened to the other one. And

speaker: FVF
words: that's what went with my money that I bought those steers with and he

speaker: FVF
words: still had his cow. (Chuckles) And oh, it was a great life. And we

speaker: FVF
words: used to do those things. And I forget- I don't just remember- my

speaker: FVF
words: brother won a coat, a mackinaw coat, from somebody that year.

speaker: SS
words: Was he betting Democrat or Republican in that year? On the president.

speaker: FVF
words: Oh, let's see, I don't even know.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, it doesn't matter.

speaker: FVF
words: No, I don't remember that.

speaker: SS
words: He won a mackinaw.

speaker: FVF
words: Yeah, he won a mackinaw. And then there was just quite a lot of, you

speaker: FVF
words: know, they bet money. Money in those days was money! (Chuckles)

speaker: FVF
words: They didn't have too much.

speaker: SS
words: You know, one thing about milking cows; now, I've never done that, it's

speaker: SS
words: almost past my time, to just be milking cows and I know some people

speaker: SS
words: still do. But when you went out to milk a cow like her, what kind of

speaker: SS
words: method did you use? I mean, did you take just a real direct approach,

speaker: SS
words: and not fool around at all?

speaker: FVF
words: We had them in stanchions. We had the cows in stanchions. Every cow

speaker: FVF
words: knew where she belonged. So we had them in these stanchions and we'd

speaker: FVF
words: usually feed 'em and then, of course, the other cows- there's a thing

speaker: FVF
words: they called a figure eight- well, a lot of times we'd put the figure

speaker: FVF
words: eight on the other cows, but this one we tried- we had these- it wa s

speaker: FVF
words: a thing that you hooked over one leg and crossed and hooked over to

speaker: FVF
words: the other leg. Well, she just kicked the thing all to pieces. You

speaker: FVF
words: couldn't put 'em on her. She wouldn't let you, she'd just fight and

speaker: FVF
words: just kick all the time, you couldn't do a thing with her. So, I just

speaker: FVF
words: quit trying to put 'em on her. I used to always, when I sit down to

speaker: FVF
words: milk, I had my milk stool and my bucket, you know, I'd sit down to milk

speaker: FVF
words: and I'd put my head in the cow's flank. If it's a small cow you can

speaker: FVF
words: put your head in a cow's flank and she can't kick you, maybe she tries

speaker: FVF
words: to kick, she can't raise her leg because I had my head in there and I

speaker: FVF
words: tried that with this cow, but it didn't work. She pretty near broke

speaker: FVF
words: my neck. So I didn't try that anymore. And I'd just have to sit there,

speaker: FVF
words: just pray that she wouldn't kick every time I went out there to milk,

speaker: FVF
words: because you never knew when she was going to kick and when she wouldn't.

speaker: FVF
words: So that was that one.

speaker: SS
words: But it wouldn't pay to try and hit her?

speaker: FVF
words: That's what they done in the first place. They whipped her. They

speaker: FVF
words: just took a rawhide, you know, one of them old blacksnakes and just,

speaker: FVF
words: oh, this man that was working for us, and he was going to whip it out

speaker: FVF
words: of her, but he never whipped it out of her, it just made her worse.

speaker: FVF
words: You just touch her with anything, you know, and whip her, why that just

speaker: FVF
words: made her worse. And she was one of these cows, she never bothered you.

speaker: FVF
words: She never done a thing to you, you could go out anywhere among her, go

speaker: FVF
words: around her, she wouldn't kick you. Just walk around where she

speaker: FVF
words: was and things like that, but she was a cow that if she was out in

speaker: FVF
words: the corral or out in the field or out in the pasture- you know, some

speaker: FVF
words: of the cows you can kind of make a run at 'em and they'll take off like

speaker: FVF
words: they're shot, but this cow, she never moved, you couldn't scare her.

speaker: FVF
words: She wasn't afraid of nothin'. You just make a run at her and she'd

speaker: FVF
words: just come right back at you.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think cows like that are fairly smart animals? I've heard people

speaker: SS
words: say that cows are just dumb, and I somehow have the idea that

speaker: SS
words: they're not as dumb as people think.

speaker: FVF
words: We had two or three cows- we got several cows from- oh, went over to

speaker: FVF
words: Harvard and got a couple of cows, they were kind of- well, it was from

speaker: FVF
words: Clarence's cousin lived over there and we went over and got these and

speaker: FVF
words: Elba land called 'em Pat and . They were good cows. Well, I could

speaker: FVF
words: take my milk pails and start for the barn and the cows'd be - after

speaker: FVF
words: we'd harvest we'd always turn the cattle in the fields, you know, and

speaker: FVF
words: I'd holler- you know, call kind of to 'em, Pat and Elba, and here they'd

speaker: FVF
words: come. Well, this one would try to beat the other one there and she'd

speaker: FVF
words: come right up to me and I could sit down and milk her anyplace^. And I

speaker: FVF
words: could milk her right in the corral, and I'd just take my bucket and

speaker: FVF
words: stool and go out there and sit down. Which ever one got there first.

speaker: FVF
words: And one would try to beat the other one. Oh, yes, they're smart. And

speaker: FVF
words: the same thing when they'd go into their stalls, they had certain

speaker: FVF
words: stalls, and one of 'em get in the wrong place and you'd just call 'em

speaker: FVF
words: by name and boy, out of there they'd get and get over there. They

speaker: FVF
words: knew where they belonged. No, no, cows are just as smart as anything

speaker: FVF
words: else if you try to teach 'em anything. Well, anything around our

speaker: FVF
words: place, the horses and cows and pigs, we could do most anything with.

speaker: SS
words: You know when you were talking about the- having a hired man; did you

speaker: SS
words: have a hired man much of the time, helping on the place?

speaker: FVF
words: No. Only in the summer, just for :work,only when someone maybe

speaker: FVF
words: wanted a place to stay. Lot of times they'd be some man been working

speaker: FVF
words: didn't have no place to stay and he'd come and say, "Well, I'll do

speaker: FVF
words: chores for,"you know, get board and bed. Yes, there've been people do

speaker: FVF
words: that lots of times.

speaker: SS
words: Would that be a neighbor man or would it be somebody that you ^know?

speaker: SS
words: Well, it would be someone that we knew, mostly. And then we did have

speaker: SS
words: a hired man quite a little bit in the spring for a long time til the

speaker: SS
words: children got big enough to help, we had ahired man in harvest and like

speaker: SS
words: that, you know, all the time. Otherwise than that we didn't have.

speaker: SS
words: Did he work with your husband mostly? Was that what he would do? Would

speaker: SS
words: he just work alongside of him?

speaker: SS
words: No, he'd have his- he'd maybe take care of the horses or, you know

speaker: SS
words: run the teams out in the field or whatever they were doing. Plowing or

speaker: SS
